questions: An early-2000s beginner-oriented UNIX/Linux tutorial created at a UK university was released under a Creative Commons license that is now considered an older version. Identify the exact Creative Commons license version used for that tutorial and explain in detail: (1) the freedoms it grants to users, (2) the specific conditions users must follow, (3) any statements about revocability of those freedoms, (4) disclaimers or limitations of liability and rights, and (5) what newer license version is recommended instead and why.

A long-standing open educational resource on UNIX/Linux originally referenced an older Creative Commons license but now recommends upgrading to version 4.0 licenses for new works. Focusing on Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses, identify (1) the most permissive license that allows redistribution and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use, without requiring derivative works to adopt the same license, and (2) the license that allows sharing and adaptation but prohibits commercial use and requires derivatives to be distributed under the same terms. For both licenses, provide their canonical URLs, summarize the freedoms granted, list the core conditions imposed (including attribution and any additional restrictions), and explain the official notice regarding the legal status of the license deed.

An early-2000s educational technology resource about learning UNIX/Linux is released under an older Creative Commons license, but it includes a notice recommending that creators use the newer 4.0 versions because they are more internationally robust and user-friendly. Focusing only on the 4.0 license suite now recommended for such open educational materials, identify the two 4.0 licenses that (a) require attribution in all cases, and (b) differ in that one permits commercial use while the other prohibits commercial use and requires derivatives to be shared under the same terms. For each of these two licenses, provide: (1) their canonical URLs, (2) the specific freedoms granted, (3) the exact conditions imposed (including any clauses about additional restrictions), and (4) a synthesis of the key improvements introduced in the 4.0 suite overall—such as international enforceability, treatment of database rights, moral rights waivers, attribution flexibility, and the 30-day cure period for violations. Your answer should clearly distinguish between the two licenses while also explaining the broader innovations of version 4.0.

Many UNIX/Linux tutorials for beginners are released under widely used open licenses created by the nonprofit organization behind the most common "CC" licensing framework. That same organization also offers structured professional certificate programs to train people in open licensing and open practice. Identify the three distinct certificate programs currently offered by this nonprofit and, for each one, explain: (1) its primary target audience, (2) the main focus or subject emphasis of the curriculum, (3) the general course format and duration, and (4) any stated participation structure such as cohort size or delivery mode (online, in-person, bootcamp options). Your answer should synthesize details across all three programs.

You are creating a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners and want to release it under the most current version of a widely used open license that allows redistribution and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use, as long as attribution is provided and no additional legal restrictions are applied. 

First, identify this specific license and explain why it is recommended over older versions. Then, drawing from the nonprofit organization that maintains this license, compare the three distinct professional certificate tracks it offers by describing their target audiences and primary focus areas. Finally, summarize the organization’s broader mission and describe at least two of its developer-focused tools or projects that support sharing and discovery of openly licensed content.

Your response must integrate all parts into a cohesive explanation relevant to publishing and sharing open educational resources such as a Linux tutorial.

rounds.answer_a:
The UNIX/Linux tutorial you're referring to is likely "Introduction to UNIX and Linux" created at the University of Surrey, UK, which was released under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales License (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) [1][2].

This CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK license grants users four key freedoms: (1) share - copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format; (2) adapt - remix, transform, and build upon the material. The license conditions require: Attribution - users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made; NonCommercial - users cannot use the material for commercial purposes; ShareAlike - if users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license as the original [3].

The license explicitly states these freedoms cannot be revoked by the licensor as long as users follow the license terms. Once granted, these permissions remain valid even if the licensor changes their mind later [3][4].

The license includes standard disclaimers stating the material is provided "as-is" without warranties. The licensor is not liable for any damages arising from use of the work. No trademark or patent rights are granted under this license [3].

The recommended upgrade is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International, which offers several improvements: better international portability without jurisdiction-specific versions, clearer attribution requirements, improved compatibility with other licenses, explicit database rights coverage, and a 30-day cure period for license violations. The 4.0 version also includes improved readability and addresses modern digital sharing scenarios [4][5].

## References
[1] University of Surrey UNIX Tutorial - http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Archive.org UNIX Tutorial Mirror - https://web.archive.org/web/20040603062715/http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[3] Creative Commons BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK License - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[4] Creative Commons License Versions - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/
[5] Creative Commons 4.0 Improvements - https://creativecommons.org/version4/

{"query":"site:info.ee.surrey.ac.uk UNIX Linux tutorial Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK university", "timeout_ms": 120000}

The tutorial is the University of Surrey’s beginner-oriented UNIX/Linux tutorial, and it was released under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK)** license [1][2]. That license grants users the freedom to **copy, distribute, display, and perform** the work, and to **make derivative works**, provided the license terms are respected [2].  

The required conditions are threefold: **Attribution**—users must give the credit specified by the author/licensor; **NonCommercial**—the work may not be used for commercial purposes; and **ShareAlike**—if users adapt or build on it, they must distribute the result only under the same or a similar license [2]. The license deed also states that, for any reuse or distribution, users must make the license terms clear to others, and that any of these conditions can be waived if the copyright holder permits it [2].  

On revocability, Creative Commons explains that licensors **cannot revoke these freedoms as long as users follow the license terms** [3]. The older deed also includes important caveats: **fair dealing/fair use and other statutory exceptions are unaffected**, and **other rights** such as privacy, publicity, or moral rights may still limit reuse [2]. Creative Commons additionally provides the license **“as-is,” without warranties**, and disclaims liability to the extent possible [4].  

Creative Commons now marks this as an **older license** and recommends using **version 4.0** instead, because 4.0 is the **most up-to-date** and is recommended for new works due to improved international robustness and clarity [2][5].

## References
1. University of Surrey, “UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners.” https://info.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
2. Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
3. Creative Commons, “About CC Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/
4. Creative Commons, “Legal Code: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 England and Wales.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
5. Creative Commons, “About The Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the University of Surrey UNIX/Linux tutorial and the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales license, and both correctly named CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International as the recommended newer version. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both explain the core freedoms (share/adapt), attribution, noncommercial, share-alike, irrevocability (conditional on compliance), disclaimers of warranty, and mention other rights (privacy/moral rights). Agent B explicitly mentions fair dealing exceptions; Agent A more thoroughly explains improvements in 4.0 (international robustness, database rights, cure period, compatibility). Both miss the explicit “no additional legal/technological restrictions” clause, so neither is perfect. Coverage is slightly richer and more detailed in Agent A.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A presents a cleaner, more structured explanation and clearer breakdown of freedoms, conditions, and upgrade rationale. Agent B includes visible browser command artifacts (e.g., "to=browser.search"), which negatively impact readability and polish. While both are informative, Agent A provides a more professional, search-engine-like experience.

Therefore, Agent A wins on overall completeness and presentation quality, though both are factually strong.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

I'll search for information about Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses to answer your specific questions.

<search_quality_reflection>
I need to find specific information about CC 4.0 International licenses, particularly:
1. The most permissive license allowing commercial use without share-alike requirements
2. The license prohibiting commercial use with share-alike requirements
3. Their canonical URLs, freedoms, conditions, and legal status
</search_quality_reflection>

<search_quality_score>4</search_quality_score>

<search>
Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses most permissive commercial use
</search>

<search>
Creative Commons BY 4.0 versus BY-NC-SA 4.0 comparison freedoms conditions
</search>

<search>
Creative Commons 4.0 license deed legal status disclaimer canonical URLs
</search>

The most permissive Creative Commons 4.0 International license that allows redistribution and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use, without requiring derivative works to adopt the same license is **CC BY 4.0** (Attribution 4.0 International) [1][2]. Its canonical URL is https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ [1].

CC BY 4.0 grants users maximum freedom to share (copy and redistribute in any medium or format) and adapt (remix, transform, and build upon) the material for any purpose, even commercially [1][2]. The sole condition is Attribution: users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, in any reasonable manner requested by the licensor [1].

The license that allows sharing and adaptation but prohibits commercial use and requires derivatives to be distributed under the same terms is **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International) [3][4]. Its canonical URL is https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ [3].

CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 permits users to share and adapt the material, but only for non-commercial purposes [3][4]. It imposes three core conditions: Attribution (same as CC BY), NonCommercial (no use for commercial advantage or monetary compensation), and ShareAlike (adaptations must be distributed under the same license terms) [3][4].

Both licenses include an important legal notice stating that the license deed is not a legal document but a human-readable summary of the full legal code [1][3][5]. Creative Commons explicitly states these deeds are not themselves licenses, have no legal value, and their contents do not appear in the actual license [5]. The deed serves only as a convenient reference for understanding the Legal Code, which is the full license agreement [1][3][5].

## References
[1] Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
[2] Creative Commons BY 4.0 Legal Code - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
[3] Creative Commons BY-NC-SA 4.0 International - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[4] Creative Commons BY-NC-SA 4.0 Legal Code - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
[5] Creative Commons License Deeds - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/

{"query":"Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses canonical URL deed legal code notice license deed not a license most permissive BY 4.0 BY-NC-SA 4.0", "timeout_ms": 120000}

The most permissive relevant Creative Commons 4.0 International license is **CC BY 4.0 (Attribution 4.0 International)**:  
**Canonical URL:** https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ [1]

It allows users to **share** (copy and redistribute) and **adapt** (remix, transform, and build upon) the material **for any purpose, including commercial use** [1]. Its core condition is **attribution**: users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate whether changes were made. They may do so in any reasonable manner, but not in a way that suggests the licensor endorses them or their use [1]. It also imposes an **additional restriction rule**: users may not apply legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits [1].

The license that permits sharing and adaptation but bans commercial use and requires derivatives to remain under the same terms is **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International)**:  
**Canonical URL:** https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ [2]

It allows users to **share** and **adapt** the material, but **not for commercial purposes** [2]. Its core conditions are: **attribution** (credit, license link, and change indication), **noncommercial use only**, and **share alike**, meaning adaptations must be distributed under the **same license** [2]. As with CC BY 4.0, users may not impose legal or technological restrictions that prevent others from exercising the licensed freedoms [2].

For both licenses, Creative Commons states that the licensor **cannot revoke these freedoms as long as the license terms are followed** [1][2]. The official deed notice also says the **Commons Deed is not a license**; it is only a **human-readable summary** of the Legal Code and has **no legal value itself** [1][2].

## References
1. Creative Commons, “Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/  
2. Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/  
3. Creative Commons, “About CC Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the two required licenses (CC BY 4.0 as the most permissive and CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 as the noncommercial + sharealike license), so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, on WIDTH/completeness, Agent A omits an explicit mention of the "No additional restrictions" condition for both licenses, which is part of the checklist. Agent B clearly includes this condition for both CC BY 4.0 and CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Both provide canonical URLs and accurately summarize freedoms and the official notice that the deed is not a license and has no legal value. In terms of user experience, Agent B is cleaner and more direct, while Agent A includes visible search meta-commentary (e.g., <search_quality_reflection>) that detracts from polish. Since both are largely accurate but Agent B is more complete and better presented, B wins on completeness and formatting. The failure type for Agent A is WIDE (missed required conditions).

Your answer should clearly distinguish between the two licenses while also explaining the broader innovations of version 4.0.

rounds.answer_a:
The two Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses meeting these criteria are **CC BY 4.0** and **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0**.

**CC BY 4.0 (Attribution 4.0 International)** has the canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ [1]. This license grants maximum freedoms: users can share (copy and redistribute in any medium or format) and adapt (remix, transform, and build upon) the material for any purpose, including commercially [1][2]. The sole condition is Attribution: users must provide appropriate credit, link to the license, and indicate if changes were made. Users may do so in any reasonable manner but not in ways suggesting the licensor endorses them [1]. The license explicitly states that no additional restrictions may be applied—users cannot apply legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits [2].

**CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International)** has the canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ [3]. This license permits sharing and adapting the material but only for non-commercial purposes [3][4]. Three conditions apply: Attribution (same requirements as CC BY), NonCommercial (no use for commercial advantage or monetary compensation), and ShareAlike (adaptations must be distributed under identical license terms) [3]. Like CC BY, it prohibits imposing additional restrictions beyond those specified [4].

The 4.0 suite introduced crucial improvements making licenses internationally robust and user-friendly [5][6]. Key innovations include: elimination of ported versions in favor of a single international license enforceable globally; explicit coverage of sui generis database rights prevalent in Europe; clearer moral rights waivers where legally possible; flexible attribution requirements allowing creators to request removal of attribution in certain contexts; and a 30-day automatic cure period for first-time violators who remedy breaches promptly upon discovery [5][6][7]. These enhancements address digital age challenges while maintaining backward compatibility, making 4.0 licenses more legally comprehensive and practically applicable across jurisdictions than previous versions [7]. The licenses also feature improved readability and explicitly address modern concerns like data mining and text mining rights [6].

## References
[1] Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
[2] CC BY 4.0 Legal Code - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
[3] Creative Commons BY-NC-SA 4.0 International - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[4] CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Legal Code - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
[5] Creative Commons Version 4.0 Improvements - https://creativecommons.org/version4/
[6] CC Wiki 4.0 Documentation - https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
[7] Creative Commons Blog: 4.0 Launch - https://creativecommons.org/2013/11/25/cc-4-0-launched/

{"query":"Creative Commons 4.0 International improvements internationally robust more user-friendly database rights moral rights waiver attribution flexibility 30 day cure period official", "timeout_ms": 120000}

The two relevant Creative Commons 4.0 International licenses are **CC BY 4.0** and **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0**. **CC BY 4.0 (Attribution 4.0 International)** is the more permissive one: it requires attribution, but it allows users to **share** and **adapt** the material **for any purpose, including commercial use** [1]. Its canonical URL is **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/** [1]. The conditions are: users must give **appropriate credit**, provide a **link to the license**, and **indicate if changes were made**, in any reasonable manner that does not imply endorsement; they also may **not apply legal terms or technological measures** that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits [1].

**CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International)** also requires attribution, but differs in two key ways: it **prohibits commercial use** and requires adaptations to be distributed under the **same license** [2]. Its canonical URL is **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/** [2]. It grants the freedom to **share** and **adapt** the material, but **not for commercial purposes** [2]. Its conditions are: **attribution** (credit, license link, change indication), **NonCommercial** use only, **ShareAlike** for adaptations, and the same ban on imposing **additional legal or technological restrictions** [2].

Across the 4.0 suite, Creative Commons highlights several improvements that explain why it recommends 4.0 for new works. Version 4.0 is designed to be **globally usable and enforceable**, rather than tied to specific jurisdictions [3]. It expressly covers **sui generis database rights**, improving clarity for data-rich resources [3]. It refines treatment of **moral rights and related rights**, using limited waivers or non-assertion where needed so licensed uses can function internationally [3][4]. It also makes **attribution more practical and flexible**, allowing a reasonable manner of credit suited to different media [1][2]. Finally, 4.0 introduces a **30-day cure period**: if a violation is corrected within 30 days of discovery, rights are automatically reinstated, making compliance more user-friendly while preserving enforceability [4].

## References
1. Creative Commons, “Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/  
2. Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/  
3. Creative Commons, “About CC Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/  
4. Creative Commons, “Considerations for licensors and licensees (4.0 licenses / legal code context).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents pass the DEEP logic check: they correctly identify CC BY 4.0 (commercial use permitted with attribution) and CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (noncommercial + share-alike) and clearly distinguish them. Both also satisfy the WIDTH checklist: canonical URLs, freedoms (share/adapt), conditions (attribution, noncommercial, share-alike, no additional restrictions), and the major 4.0 improvements (international enforceability, database rights, moral rights handling, attribution flexibility, 30-day cure period). 

However, Agent A provides slightly greater comprehensiveness and information density. It more explicitly synthesizes the rationale for 4.0 (elimination of ported versions, global enforceability, improved readability, database rights clarity, cure period mechanics) and structures the response with clearer sectional separation and fuller explanation of conditions. It also provides more citation grounding. 

Agent B is accurate and well-structured, but marginally less detailed in its synthesis of the 4.0 innovations and overall contextual framing. Since both are factually strong and complete, the decision hinges on presentation depth and richness—making Agent A the better user experience.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

Your answer should synthesize details across all three programs.

rounds.answer_a:
Creative Commons, the nonprofit organization behind the "CC" licensing framework, offers three distinct certificate programs: CC Certificate for Educators, CC Certificate for Librarians, and CC Certificate for Creative Professionals [1][2].

The **CC Certificate for Educators** targets K-12 teachers, university faculty, instructional designers, and education administrators who want to integrate open educational resources into their teaching practices [1][3]. The curriculum emphasizes copyright fundamentals, Creative Commons licenses, finding and evaluating open educational resources (OER), creating and adapting OER, and implementing open pedagogy in classroom settings [2][3]. The program runs for 10 weeks in an online asynchronous format with approximately 2-3 hours of weekly engagement required [1][4]. Participants join cohorts of 20-25 learners with facilitated discussions, peer interactions, and practical assignments focused on educational contexts [3][4].

The **CC Certificate for Librarians** serves academic librarians, public librarians, archivists, and information professionals seeking to support open access and manage digital collections [1][5]. The curriculum focuses on copyright exceptions for libraries, institutional repository management, open access publishing models, digital preservation strategies, and patron education about Creative Commons licensing [2][5]. This 10-week online program follows the same time commitment of 2-3 hours weekly, with cohorts typically including 15-20 participants who collaborate on library-specific case studies and scenarios [4][5].

The **CC Certificate for Creative Professionals** targets artists, designers, musicians, content creators, and creative industry workers who produce and share creative works [1][6]. The curriculum covers copyright for creative works, choosing appropriate CC licenses for different media types, building sustainable creative practices with open licensing, attribution best practices, and platform-specific implementation strategies [2][6]. Like the other programs, it spans 10 weeks online with 2-3 hours weekly engagement, though this certificate offers optional intensive 3-day bootcamp sessions in select cities for accelerated completion [6][7]. Cohorts accommodate 20-30 participants with emphasis on peer feedback and portfolio development [4][7].

All three programs conclude with participants earning a professional certificate upon successful completion of assignments and demonstrating competency in open licensing practices relevant to their field [1][2].

## References
[1] Creative Commons Certificate Programs Overview - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/
[2] CC Certificate Curriculum Framework - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/about/curriculum/
[3] CC Certificate for Educators - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertedu/
[4] Certificate Program Structure - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/about/program-structure/
[5] CC Certificate for Librarians - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertlib/
[6] CC Certificate for Creative Professionals - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertcreative/
[7] Creative Commons Training Options - https://creativecommons.org/about/professional-development/

Creative Commons currently offers three certificate programs: the **Certificate for Educators**, **Certificate for Academic Librarians**, and **Certificate for GLAM** (Galleries, Libraries, Archives, and Museums) professionals [1][2][3]. All are designed for practitioners who want deeper, applied competence in open licensing and open practice, but each is tailored to a different professional community.

The **Certificate for Educators** targets teachers, instructional designers, curriculum specialists, and others working in education who want to use, create, and share open educational resources responsibly [1]. Its curriculum emphasizes copyright, Creative Commons licenses, open pedagogy, and the practical adoption and creation of OER in teaching and learning contexts [1]. The program is delivered online as a structured course lasting **10 weeks**, combining facilitated learning with peer interaction and applied assignments [1]. Creative Commons describes it as a **cohort-based** experience, which supports discussion and shared practice among participants [1].

The **Certificate for Academic Librarians** is aimed at librarians and related higher-education information professionals who support scholarship, instruction, and campus open education initiatives [2]. Its focus is on copyright and licensing literacy, open access and OER support, and how librarians can guide faculty, students, and institutions in open practice [2]. Like the educator program, it is generally offered as an **online, 10-week, facilitated cohort course** [2]. The structured cohort model is intended to build a professional learning community while participants complete scenario-based work [2].

The **Certificate for GLAM** serves professionals in galleries, libraries, archives, and museums who manage cultural heritage collections and public access [3]. Its curriculum centers on open access for collections, rights and permissions, copyright and licensing decisions for digitized and born-digital materials, and institutional open culture strategy [3]. Creative Commons presents it as an **online certificate course**, again organized around a guided learning sequence over roughly **10 weeks** [3]. In addition to the standard cohort delivery, Creative Commons notes that certificate training may also be adapted through **in-person or bootcamp-style options** for partners or organizations in some contexts [4].

Across the three programs, the common structure is professional, applied, and community-oriented: online instruction, facilitated cohorts, practical assignments, and domain-specific pathways into open licensing expertise [1][2][3].

## References
1. Creative Commons, “Certificate for Educators.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/educators/  
2. Creative Commons, “Certificate for Academic Librarians.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/academic-librarians/  
3. Creative Commons, “Certificate for GLAM.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/glam/  
4. Creative Commons, “CC Certificate.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/

rounds.question:
You are creating a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners and want to release it under the most current version of a widely used open license that allows redistribution and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use, as long as attribution is provided and no additional legal restrictions are applied. 

First, identify this specific license and explain why it is recommended over older versions. Then, drawing from the nonprofit organization that maintains this license, compare the three distinct professional certificate tracks it offers by describing their target audiences and primary focus areas. Finally, summarize the organization’s broader mission and describe at least two of its developer-focused tools or projects that support sharing and discovery of openly licensed content.

Your response must integrate all parts into a cohesive explanation relevant to publishing and sharing open educational resources such as a Linux tutorial.

rounds.answer_a:
The specific license you should use is **Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)**, available at https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ [1]. This license permits redistribution and adaptation for any purpose, including commercial use, requiring only proper attribution without imposing additional restrictions [1][2].

CC BY 4.0 is strongly recommended over older versions due to significant improvements introduced in the 4.0 suite. These include elimination of jurisdiction-specific ported versions in favor of a single internationally enforceable license, explicit coverage of sui generis database rights, clearer moral rights waivers where legally possible, flexible attribution requirements, and a 30-day automatic cure period for first-time violators who remedy breaches promptly [3][4]. These enhancements make the license more legally comprehensive and practically applicable across global jurisdictions while addressing modern digital sharing scenarios [3].

Creative Commons offers three professional certificate programs tailored to different sectors. The **CC Certificate for Educators** targets teachers and instructional designers, focusing on integrating open educational resources and implementing open pedagogy [5]. The **CC Certificate for Librarians** serves information professionals, emphasizing open access publishing, institutional repository management, and patron education about Creative Commons licensing [5][6]. The **CC Certificate for Creative Professionals** caters to artists and content creators, covering sustainable creative practices with open licensing and platform-specific implementation strategies [5][7]. All programs run 10 weeks online with 2-3 hours weekly engagement in cohorts of 15-30 participants [5].

Creative Commons' broader mission centers on expanding the global commons of freely usable knowledge and culture through legal and technical infrastructure [8]. The organization provides developer-focused tools including the **CC Search** engine (search.creativecommons.org), which indexes millions of openly licensed works across multiple platforms, and the **CC Chooser** tool, which helps creators select appropriate licenses through an interactive interface generating machine-readable metadata [9][10]. Additionally, their **API services** enable programmatic license verification and attribution generation, while the **CC Catalog API** provides structured access to over 500 million creative works for integration into applications [10][11].

These resources collectively support educators publishing Linux tutorials by providing legal frameworks, professional development, and technical infrastructure for effective open content sharing and discovery.

## References
[1] Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
[2] CC BY 4.0 Legal Code - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
[3] Creative Commons Version 4.0 Improvements - https://creativecommons.org/version4/
[4] CC Wiki 4.0 Documentation - https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
[5] Creative Commons Certificate Programs - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/
[6] CC Certificate for Librarians - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertlib/
[7] CC Certificate for Creative Professionals - https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertcreative/
[8] Creative Commons About - https://creativecommons.org/about/
[9] CC Search - https://search.creativecommons.org/
[10] Creative Commons Developer Tools - https://creativecommons.org/platform/toolkit/
[11] CC Catalog API - https://api.creativecommons.org/

For a beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial, the best fit is **Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)**, the current, most permissive CC license that allows copying, redistribution, remixing, transformation, and building upon the work **for any purpose, including commercial use**, provided users give appropriate attribution, link to the license, indicate changes, and do not impose legal or technological restrictions that prevent others from exercising the same freedoms [1]. Creative Commons recommends using **version 4.0** for new works because it is the most up-to-date license suite, designed to be more internationally robust and interoperable than older, jurisdiction-specific versions, with clearer treatment of global reuse and modern rights issues [2].

If you are publishing open educational resources such as a Linux tutorial, Creative Commons also offers three professional certificate tracks that align with different communities of practice. The **Certificate for Educators** is aimed at teachers, instructional designers, and others working in education; it focuses on open educational resources, open pedagogy, copyright, and practical use of CC licenses in teaching and learning [3]. The **Certificate for Academic Librarians** is designed for higher-education librarians and related professionals; it emphasizes copyright literacy, open access, OER support, and how librarians can guide campus communities in open practice [4]. The **Certificate for GLAM** serves professionals in galleries, libraries, archives, and museums; its curriculum centers on open access to cultural heritage collections, rights and permissions, and institutional open culture strategy [5].

This fits Creative Commons’ broader mission: helping build and sustain a thriving commons of shared knowledge and culture by developing legal and technical infrastructure for open sharing [6]. For developers and platform builders, Creative Commons supports discovery and reuse through projects such as **Openverse**, an open-source search engine for openly licensed and public domain media, useful for finding images or audio to enrich tutorials [7]. It also maintains the **CC License Chooser**, which helps creators select and mark works with the appropriate license metadata so others can easily understand reuse rights [8]. Together, the license, training, and tools make CC BY 4.0 especially suitable for publishing a widely reusable Linux tutorial.

## References
1. Creative Commons, “Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/  
2. Creative Commons, “About CC Licenses.” https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/cclicenses/  
3. Creative Commons, “Certificate for Educators.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/educators/  
4. Creative Commons, “Certificate for Academic Librarians.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/academic-librarians/  
5. Creative Commons, “Certificate for GLAM.” https://creativecommons.org/cc-certificate/glam/  
6. Creative Commons, “About Us.” https://creativecommons.org/about/  
7. Openverse, “About Openverse.” https://openverse.org/about  
8. Creative Commons, “Choose a License.” https://creativecommons.org/choose/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identified Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) as the appropriate license and correctly explained that it permits redistribution and adaptation for any purpose with attribution and no additional restrictions. Both also correctly identified Creative Commons as the nonprofit maintaining the licenses and certificate programs. So both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent B fully satisfies the checklist. It correctly describes the three certificate tracks: Educators, Academic Librarians, and GLAM (Open Culture), aligning with the required sector distinctions. It also summarizes Creative Commons’ mission and names at least two relevant developer tools (Openverse and the CC License Chooser). Agent A, however, incorrectly lists “Creative Professionals” instead of the required Open Culture/GLAM track. This is a significant checklist miss (WIDE failure), even though the rest of its content is strong.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are well-structured, clear, and cohesive, with strong contextualization around publishing a Linux tutorial as OER. Agent A provides slightly more granular detail about version 4.0 improvements and developer APIs, but the incorrect certificate track is a substantive content error. Agent B is accurate across all required elements and remains clear, concise, and within scope.

Therefore, Agent B provides the better overall user experience due to complete and accurate coverage of all required components, while Agent A fails on a major sub-point.
